runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age U.S. workers for a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed.
citations: Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001) – lottery-winner study estimating income effect on hours; found negative but modest income elasticity around -0.1 to -0.2
Pencavel (1986) – handbook chapter surveying male labor supply; income elasticities typically small and negative, roughly -0.1
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) – labor supply handbook chapter; intensive-margin income elasticities generally small and negative for prime-age workers
runs.reasoningSummary: The standard theoretical prediction is a negative income effect on hours: leisure is a normal good, so higher non-labor income reduces labor supply. Empirical estimates for prime-age employed workers in the U.S. are consistently small and negative. Lottery and inheritance studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) find income elasticities around -0.1 to -0.2. Structural labor supply literature (Pencavel, Blundell & MaCurdy) similarly finds modest negative intensive-margin income effects, often in the -0.05 to -0.20 range. There is some heterogeneity across subgroups and methods, and a small number of studies find near-zero or slightly positive estimates, so I place modest probability on values near zero or slightly positive at the upper tail. My median is -0.10, with the 90% credible interval spanning roughly -0.35 to +0.02.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Income elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Sign convention for this quantity:
- A positive elasticity means individuals work more when they have more resources.
- A negative elasticity means individuals work less when they have more resources.
- Under the definition above, if additional non-labor income reduces annual hours worked, the elasticity should be negative.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age U.S. workers for a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed.
citations: Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001) - lottery winners study finding small negative income effects on hours
MaCurdy (1981) - lifecycle labor supply model estimating income effect parameters
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) - survey of labor supply elasticities noting income effects tend to be small and negative
runs.reasoningSummary: The income effect on hours worked (intensive margin) for employed prime-age workers is theoretically negative under standard preferences (leisure is a normal good). Empirical estimates from quasi-experimental studies (e.g., lottery windfalls, inheritances) and structural lifecycle models consistently find small negative income elasticities on the intensive margin, typically in the range of -0.05 to -0.20. The effect is generally modest because employed workers face constraints (standard hours norms, employer-set schedules) that limit their ability to reduce hours in response to income gains. I center my belief around -0.10, with uncertainty skewed slightly toward zero and with a small positive tail reflecting the possibility of near-zero or slightly positive estimates from some studies.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: percent change in hours worked per year for prime-age U.S. workers already employed, given a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed.
citations: MaCurdy (1981) – structural lifecycle labor supply estimates suggest small negative income effects on hours, roughly -0.1 to -0.2
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999, Handbook of Labor Economics) – survey noting income elasticity of hours typically in range -0.1 to -0.3 for men, smaller in magnitude for women on intensive margin
Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001, AER) – lottery-winner study finds negative but modest income effect on hours worked
runs.reasoningSummary: The income effect on hours worked (intensive margin) is theoretically negative under standard preferences—more non-labor income raises the demand for leisure (a normal good), reducing hours. Empirical estimates for prime-age U.S. workers cluster around -0.05 to -0.20, with most structural estimates for men near -0.1 and somewhat similar or smaller in magnitude for women on the intensive margin. Lottery/windfall studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) tend to find effects in a similar small-negative range. A small positive value is not impossible if estimates are near zero with measurement error, but the central tendency is clearly negative and small. I place the median at -0.10, with most of the distribution between -0.18 and -0.04.

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Income elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Sign convention for this quantity:
- A positive elasticity means individuals work more when they have more resources.
- A negative elasticity means individuals work less when they have more resources.
- Under the definition above, if additional non-labor income reduces annual hours worked, the elasticity should be negative.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity with respect to non-labor income, conditional on employment, for prime-age U.S. workers, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed.
citations: Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) – survey of labor supply elasticities noting small negative income effects on hours for workers already employed
Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001) – lottery-winner study finding modest negative income effects on hours worked
Pencavel (1986) – handbook chapter on male labor supply with income elasticity estimates in the -0.1 to -0.2 range
runs.reasoningSummary: The theoretical prediction from standard utility theory is that leisure is a normal good, so higher non-labor income should reduce hours worked (negative elasticity). Empirical estimates for the intensive margin—changes in hours among those already working—tend to be small in magnitude. Lottery and dividend studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) suggest modest negative effects. Handbook surveys (Blundell & MaCurdy 1999; Pencavel 1986) place intensive-margin income elasticities roughly between -0.05 and -0.2 for prime-age workers. I center my belief around -0.1, with substantial uncertainty given heterogeneity across studies and identification strategies, but I keep the distribution almost entirely in negative territory consistent with theory and the bulk of evidence.

runs.reasoningSummary: The theoretical prediction under standard preferences is a negative income effect: higher non-labor income reduces hours worked (leisure is a normal good). Empirical estimates for prime-age employed workers in the U.S. consistently find small negative intensive-margin income elasticities. Lottery and windfall studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) suggest values around -0.1 to -0.2. Structural labor-supply estimates (Pencavel, Blundell & MaCurdy) cluster in a similar range, often -0.05 to -0.20. Very few credible estimates are positive or larger in magnitude than -0.3. I center my median at -0.10, with uncertainty spanning roughly -0.35 to +0.02 at the 5th and 95th percentiles.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: the percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age U.S. workers for a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
citations: Imbens, Rubin, and Sacerdote (2001) – lottery winners study finding negative but modest income effects on hours worked
MaCurdy (1981) – life-cycle labor supply model estimating small negative income effects on intensive margin
Blundell and MaCurdy (1999) – survey of labor supply elasticities noting income effects on hours typically small and negative, around -0.1 to -0.2
runs.reasoningSummary: The income elasticity of labor supply on the intensive margin for employed prime-age U.S. workers is generally estimated to be small and negative, consistent with leisure being a normal good. Lottery and windfall studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) find modest reductions in hours from unearned income. Structural estimates from life-cycle models (MaCurdy 1981, Blundell and MaCurdy 1999) typically place this elasticity in the range of roughly -0.05 to -0.20. There is some uncertainty—a handful of studies find near-zero effects, and a few quasi-experimental designs yield slightly more negative estimates—but the central tendency is around -0.10. The distribution is skewed toward negative values, with very little probability mass on positive values (which would imply hours rise with non-labor income, contrary to standard theory and most evidence).

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment: percent change in annual hours worked by already-employed prime-age U.S. workers for a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
citations: Blundell, R. & MaCurdy, T. (1999). Labor supply: A review of alternative approaches. Handbook of Labor Economics, Vol. 3A. — Classic review summarizing income elasticity estimates typically in the range of −0.1 to −0.3 for hours conditional on work.

MaCurdy, T. (1981). An empirical model of labor supply in a life-cycle setting. Journal of Political Economy, 89(6). — Structural life-cycle model; income elasticity estimated close to zero or modestly negative.

Keane, M. (2011). Labor supply and taxes: A survey. Journal of Economic Literature, 49(4). — Surveys indicate intensive-margin income elasticities are small and negative, generally between 0 and −0.2.
runs.reasoningSummary: The income elasticity of labor supply on the intensive margin is theoretically expected to be negative (leisure is a normal good), but empirical estimates for prime-age workers are typically small in magnitude. The Blundell-MaCurdy handbook survey and Keane's JEL survey both place intensive-margin income elasticities in a narrow range around −0.1 to −0.2 for men, with some studies closer to zero. Women tend to show somewhat larger magnitudes but still modest. Given the target population (prime-age, already employed, U.S.), I center my belief at −0.10, with meaningful probability of values between −0.20 and near zero, and small but nonzero probability of a positive estimate (reflecting noisy empirical estimates or special subgroups).

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity with respect to non-labor income, conditional on employment, for prime-age U.S. workers — i.e., the percent change in hours worked per year for a 1% increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
citations: MaCurdy (1981) — structural lifecycle labor supply model estimates small negative income effects on hours for prime-age men
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) — survey of labor supply elasticities, income effects on hours generally small and negative, often in range -0.1 to -0.3 for men
Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001) — lottery-winner study finds negative but modest income effects on hours worked
runs.reasoningSummary: The income elasticity of labor supply on the intensive margin is theoretically negative under standard preferences (leisure is a normal good): higher non-labor income raises the demand for leisure and reduces hours worked. Empirical estimates for prime-age U.S. workers are consistently small in magnitude. Male workers show income elasticities close to zero to modestly negative (roughly -0.05 to -0.20 in many structural estimates). Female workers tend to show larger negative income effects but the target population is prime-age workers broadly. Lottery and transfer studies (e.g., Imbens et al.) suggest small reductions in hours. I center my belief at about -0.10, with substantial uncertainty spanning roughly -0.35 to near zero, and a small tail probability of a slightly positive estimate (consistent with some specifications finding near-zero or ambiguous results).

Quantity of interest:
- Name: Income elasticity of labor supply
- Definition: Percent change in annual hours worked from a 1 percent increase in non-labor income, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed and excluding participation responses.
- Target interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity conditional on employment
- Population/context: Prime-age workers in the United States who are already working
- Units: elasticity

Sign convention for this quantity:
- A positive elasticity means individuals work more when they have more resources.
- A negative elasticity means individuals work less when they have more resources.
- Under the definition above, if additional non-labor income reduces annual hours worked, the elasticity should be negative.

runs.interpretation: Intensive-margin annual-hours elasticity with respect to non-labor income, conditional on employment, for prime-age U.S. workers, holding the net-of-tax wage fixed.
citations: Imbens, Rubin & Sacerdote (2001) — lottery-winner study finding small negative income effects on hours worked among employed individuals
Pencavel (1986) — handbook chapter surveying male labor supply, reporting income elasticities generally between -0.1 and -0.3
Blundell & MaCurdy (1999) — handbook chapter on labor supply, summarizing intensive-margin income elasticities clustering near zero to slightly negative
runs.reasoningSummary: The income effect on intensive-margin hours worked is theoretically negative (leisure is a normal good), but empirically small in magnitude for prime-age employed workers. Lottery and windfall studies (e.g., Imbens et al. 2001) find small negative effects on hours, and structural labor-supply surveys (Pencavel 1986; Blundell & MaCurdy 1999) place the intensive-margin income elasticity in roughly the -0.05 to -0.20 range for this population. The distribution is centered around -0.10, with meaningful uncertainty toward more negative values and a small probability of near-zero or trivially positive estimates from noisy identification.
